title: JavaScript
summary: A to-the-point review of the JavaScript an SDET gets asked about: types & coercion, var/let/const, scope, closures, this, prototypes & classes, the event loop, promises & async/await, and array methods — plus the Jest / Playwright testing stack. Short explanations, short examples.

sections.title: Types & Variables
sections.bodyHtml:
JavaScript is dynamically typed: variables don’t have types, values do. There are 7 primitives and everything else is an object.

  • Primitives (immutable, copied by value): string, number, boolean, null, undefined, bigint, symbol.
  • Objects (arrays, functions, plain objects) are reference types — assigning copies the reference, so both names point at the same object.
  • typeof reports the type — with two classic quirks: typeof null === "object" (a historic bug) and typeof function(){} === "function".
let a = 1; let b = a; b = 9;          // a stays 1 (value copied)

let x = [1]; let y = x; y[0] = 9;     // x[0] is now 9 (same array)

typeof undefined  // "undefined"
typeof null       // "object"  (quirk)
NaN === NaN       // false — use Number.isNaN()
null vs undefined: undefined = a variable was declared but never assigned (the engine’s default); null = you deliberately set “no value”.

sections.title: var, let & const
sections.bodyHtml:
KeywordScopeReassign?Hoisting
varfunctionyeshoisted, init undefined
letblockyeshoisted but in the TDZ
constblocknohoisted but in the TDZ
  • Default to const; use let only when you reassign; avoid var.
  • const stops reassignment, not mutation — const o = {}; o.x = 1 is fine.
  • TDZ (temporal dead zone): a let/const exists but can’t be read until its declaration runs — touching it early throws, unlike var which reads as undefined.
const cfg = { retries: 3 };
cfg.retries = 5;     // OK — mutating
cfg = {};            // TypeError — reassigning a const

sections.title: Scope, Hoisting & Closures
sections.bodyHtml:
Hoisting: declarations are processed before code runs. function declarations are fully hoisted (callable above their definition); var is hoisted as undefined; let/const are hoisted into the TDZ.

A closure is a function that remembers the variables from the scope where it was created, even after that scope has returned. It’s the backbone of private state and the single most-asked JS concept.

function counter() {
  let n = 0;                 // captured by the closure
  return () => ++n;          // remembers n after counter() returns
}
const next = counter();
next(); next();              // 1, then 2

// classic interview bug — var is function-scoped:
for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) setTimeout(() => console.log(i));  // 3 3 3
for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) setTimeout(() => console.log(i));  // 0 1 2
Why let fixes the loop: let creates a fresh binding per iteration, so each callback closes over its own i; var shares one binding.

sections.title: this & Function Binding
sections.bodyHtml:
this is decided by how a function is called, not where it’s defined — except arrow functions.

  • Method call obj.fn() — this is obj.
  • Plain call fn() — this is undefined in strict mode (else the global object).
  • Arrow function — has no own this; it inherits from the enclosing scope. Ideal for callbacks.
  • call/apply invoke with an explicit this; bind returns a new function permanently bound.
const user = {
  name: "Ada",
  greetLater() {
    setTimeout(() => console.log(this.name), 0);  // arrow keeps `this` = user
  }
};
user.greetLater();   // "Ada"

const loose = user.greetLater;
// loose();          // a plain `function` callback here would lose `this`

sections.title: Prototypes & Classes
sections.bodyHtml:
JS inheritance is prototype-based: every object has a hidden link to a prototype object, and property lookups walk up that prototype chain until found or it hits null.

class (ES6) is syntactic sugar over prototypes — cleaner syntax, same mechanism.

class Animal {
  constructor(name) { this.name = name; }
  speak() { return `${this.name} makes a sound`; }
}
class Dog extends Animal {
  speak() { return `${this.name} barks`; }   // override
}
new Dog("Rex").speak();   // "Rex barks"

// equivalent prototype lookup:
Object.getPrototypeOf(new Dog("Rex")) === Dog.prototype;  // true
Say this: classes don’t add a new inheritance model — they’re sugar over the prototype chain that already existed.

sections.title: Arrays, Objects & Modern Syntax
sections.bodyHtml:
• Immutable iteration — map, filter, reduce, find, some/every return new values without mutating the source.
  • Destructuring — pull fields out of objects/arrays in one line.
  • Spread / rest (...) — copy & merge, or gather arguments.
  • Optional chaining ?. and nullish coalescing ?? for safe access and defaults (?? only falls back on null/undefined, unlike ||).
const users = [{ id: 1, role: "admin" }, { id: 2, role: "user" }];
const admins = users.filter(u => u.role === "admin").map(u => u.id);

const { id, role = "guest" } = users[0];      // destructure + default
const merged = { ...users[0], active: true };  // spread copy

const city = order?.address?.city ?? "unknown";  // safe + fallback
?? vs ||: 0 || 5 is 5 (0 is falsy) but 0 ?? 5 is 0 — use ?? when 0/""/false are valid values.

sections.title: The Event Loop
sections.bodyHtml:
JS is single-threaded. The event loop is how it does non-blocking work: synchronous code runs on the call stack; async callbacks wait in queues and run only when the stack is empty.

  • Call stack — runs synchronous code now.
  • Microtask queue — resolved promise callbacks (.then, await continuations). Drained fully after each task, before any macrotask.
  • Macrotask queue — setTimeout, I/O, events. One per loop turn.
console.log(1);
setTimeout(() => console.log(2), 0);   // macrotask
Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log(3)); // microtask
console.log(4);
// prints 1, 4, 3, 2  — microtasks beat the timer
Why 3 before 2: the promise callback is a microtask and runs before the next macrotask (the timer), even with a 0 ms delay.

sections.title: Promises & async / await
sections.bodyHtml:
A Promise represents a future value: pending → fulfilled | rejected. async/await is syntax over promises that reads like synchronous code.

  • await pauses the async function until the promise settles, without blocking the thread.
  • Wrap await in try/catch to handle rejections (the equivalent of .catch).
  • Promise.all runs work concurrently and fails fast; Promise.allSettled waits for all regardless; Promise.race takes the first to settle.
  • Don’t await in a loop when calls are independent — fire them together with Promise.all.
async function loadUser(id) {
  try {
    const res = await fetch(`/users/${id}`);
    if (!res.ok) throw new Error(res.status);
    return await res.json();
  } catch (e) {
    console.error("load failed", e);
    throw e;
  }
}

// concurrent, not sequential:
const [a, b] = await Promise.all([loadUser(1), loadUser(2)]);

sections.title: Test Stack for SDETs
sections.bodyHtml:
For a Playwright/SDET role the JS testing surface is small and worth naming:

  • Playwright Test — the runner you’ll use most: test/expect, fixtures, auto-waiting web-first assertions, parallel projects, trace viewer.
  • Jest / Vitest — unit testing: describe/it/expect, mocks & spies (jest.fn(), jest.mock()), snapshots.
  • Cypress — alternative E2E framework; in-browser, good DX, retry-able assertions.
  • TypeScript — most modern test suites are TS for type-safe page objects and fixtures.
import { test, expect } from '@playwright/test';

test('user can sign in', async ({ page }) => {
  await page.goto('/login');
  await page.getByLabel('Email').fill('ada@test.dev');
  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Sign in' }).click();
  await expect(page.getByText('Welcome')).toBeVisible(); // auto-retries
});

quiz.question: == vs ===?
choices: === compares value and type with no coercion; == coerces types first, causing surprises
== compares value and type strictly; === also checks the variable's memory address
=== and == are identical; === is just the older, deprecated spelling
== never coerces but === converts both operands to strings before comparing
quiz.correctAnswer: === compares value and type with no coercion; == coerces types first, causing surprises
quiz.explanation: === compares value and type with no coercion (always prefer it); == coerces types first, giving surprises like 0 == "" and null == undefined being true.

quiz.question: var vs let vs const?
choices: All three are block-scoped; only var can be reassigned after declaration
var is block-scoped and hoisted; let/const are function-scoped with no TDZ
var is function-scoped and hoisted as undefined; let/const are block-scoped with a TDZ and const can't be reassigned
const is function-scoped and freezes objects; let and var are both block-scoped
quiz.correctAnswer: var is function-scoped and hoisted as undefined; let/const are block-scoped with a TDZ and const can't be reassigned
quiz.explanation: var is function-scoped and hoisted as undefined; let/const are block-scoped with a temporal dead zone; const can’t be reassigned (but objects can still mutate).

quiz.question: What is a closure?
choices: A function that copies all outer variables by value at the moment it is defined
A function bound to an object so its this never changes when called
A function that retains access to variables from the scope where it was created, even after that scope returns
An object that hides its properties so they cannot be enumerated or read
quiz.correctAnswer: A function that retains access to variables from the scope where it was created, even after that scope returns
quiz.explanation: A function that retains access to variables from the scope where it was created, even after that scope returns — the basis for private state.

quiz.question: How is `this` determined?
choices: By where the function is defined; arrow functions get their own fresh this
By the call site: obj.fn() → obj, plain fn() → undefined in strict mode, arrows inherit from the enclosing scope
Always the global object unless the function was created with the new keyword
By the first argument passed, which becomes this inside every function call
quiz.correctAnswer: By the call site: obj.fn() → obj, plain fn() → undefined in strict mode, arrows inherit from the enclosing scope
quiz.explanation: By the call site: obj.fn() → obj; plain fn() → undefined in strict mode; arrow functions have no own this and inherit it from the enclosing scope.

quiz.question: null vs undefined?
choices: undefined is an intentional empty value; null is the engine's default for unassigned
Both mean the same thing; null is just the older keyword for undefined
undefined = declared but unassigned (engine default); null = an intentional 'no value' you assign
undefined means the variable was never declared; null means it was deleted
quiz.correctAnswer: undefined = declared but unassigned (engine default); null = an intentional 'no value' you assign
quiz.explanation: undefined = declared but unassigned (engine default); null = an intentional 'no value' you assign.

quiz.question: What is the event loop?
choices: A background thread that runs async callbacks in parallel with main code
Single thread runs sync code on the call stack; when empty it drains all microtasks then takes one macrotask per turn
A queue that runs one microtask and one macrotask alternately each turn
A scheduler that prioritizes setTimeout callbacks over resolved promises
quiz.correctAnswer: Single thread runs sync code on the call stack; when empty it drains all microtasks then takes one macrotask per turn
quiz.explanation: Single thread runs sync code on the call stack; when empty it drains all microtasks (promises) then takes one macrotask (setTimeout/IO) per turn.

quiz.question: Microtask vs macrotask?
choices: Macrotasks run before microtasks, so setTimeout(…,0) beats a resolved promise
Microtasks and macrotasks share one queue and run in registration order
Promise callbacks are microtasks and run before the next macrotask, so a resolved promise beats setTimeout(…, 0)
Microtasks only run after all macrotasks (timers, IO) have been processed
quiz.correctAnswer: Promise callbacks are microtasks and run before the next macrotask, so a resolved promise beats setTimeout(…, 0)
quiz.explanation: Promise callbacks are microtasks and run before the next macrotask (timers, IO) — so a resolved promise beats setTimeout(…, 0).

quiz.question: Promise.all vs allSettled?
choices: all runs concurrently and rejects on the first failure; allSettled waits for every promise and reports each outcome
all waits for every promise regardless; allSettled rejects on the first failure
all runs promises sequentially; allSettled runs them concurrently and fails fast
both reject on the first failure, but allSettled also returns the rejection reasons
quiz.correctAnswer: all runs concurrently and rejects on the first failure; allSettled waits for every promise and reports each outcome
quiz.explanation: all runs concurrently and rejects on the first failure; allSettled waits for every promise and reports each outcome.

quiz.question: ?? vs ||?
choices: || falls back on any falsy value (0, '', false); ?? falls back only on null/undefined
?? falls back on any falsy value; || falls back only on null/undefined
both fall back only on null/undefined, but ?? also treats NaN as nullish
|| falls back only on undefined; ?? falls back on null, undefined, and 0
quiz.correctAnswer: || falls back on any falsy value (0, '', false); ?? falls back only on null/undefined
quiz.explanation: || falls back on any falsy value (0, '', false); ?? falls back only on null/undefined — use ?? when 0/''/false are valid.

quiz.question: Are JS classes real classes?
choices: Yes — ES6 classes introduced a new class-based inheritance model alongside prototypes
No — syntactic sugar over the prototype chain; extends/super just wire up prototype links
Yes — class instances no longer use prototypes, only fixed property slots
No — classes are sugar over closures that store private fields internally
quiz.correctAnswer: No — syntactic sugar over the prototype chain; extends/super just wire up prototype links
quiz.explanation: No — syntactic sugar over the prototype chain; extends/super just wire up prototype links.

quiz.question: How do you avoid the classic loop-var bug?
choices: Wrap the loop body in a setTimeout so each callback fires after the loop ends
Use let (fresh binding per iteration) instead of var, so each callback closes over its own value
Declare the loop variable with var outside the loop to give it a stable address
Use const for the loop counter so it can never be reassigned mid-iteration
quiz.correctAnswer: Use let (fresh binding per iteration) instead of var, so each callback closes over its own value
quiz.explanation: Use let (fresh binding per iteration) instead of var, so each callback closes over its own value.

quiz.question: Shallow vs deep copy?
choices: Spread copies all nested levels; deep copy only duplicates the top level
JSON.stringify makes a shallow copy; spread makes a full deep copy
Both spread and structuredClone copy one level; deep copy requires Object.freeze
Spread/Object.assign copy one level (nested objects stay shared); deep copy needs structuredClone() or a recursive clone
quiz.correctAnswer: Spread/Object.assign copy one level (nested objects stay shared); deep copy needs structuredClone() or a recursive clone
quiz.explanation: Spread/Object.assign copy one level (nested objects stay shared); deep copy needs structuredClone() or a recursive clone.
